entry.title: Building an Autonomous SAP Joule Agent for ServiceNow ITSM on SAP BTP
entry.content:
Moving on from the two very basic custom Joule Skills created in the previous post — which could only create and update ServiceNow incidents with a very limited and hardcoded set of input values — this blog takes a much bigger leap toward a fully autonomous custom SAP Joule Agent.

This custom AI-powered Joule Agent, which I call ServiceNow ITSM Expert, leverages the power of an MCP server built for ServiceNow using OpenAPI specifications for ServiceNow APIs and deployed on SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP).

The agent provides all the capabilities required to interact intelligently with ServiceNow incidents, including:

Creating incidents
Updating incidents
Searching incidents
Listing incidents
Copying incidents
Maintaining incident quality and categorization automatically

The simplified architecture looks like this:

 

While the technical integration itself is powerful, the real focus of this Joule Agent is something much more important: maintaining incident quality and proper categorization for operational reporting purposes.

The Main Idea Behind the Agent

Traditional incident creation processes often rely heavily on users manually selecting categories, configuration items, and custom classification fields. This leads to inconsistent reporting, incorrect categorization, and operational inefficiencies.

The ServiceNow ITSM Expert agent solves this problem by intelligently determining the correct values using:

The incident description and business context provided by the user
ITSM best practices
Historical incident analysis
Real-time ServiceNow data retrieval
AI reasoning powered by SAP Joule

Instead of assigning random or hardcoded values, the agent dynamically fetches and validates field values directly from ServiceNow before creating or updating incidents.

This logic is accurately handled through the MCP server running on SAP BTP, which acts as the intelligent middleware layer between SAP Joule and ServiceNow.

Intelligent Incident Categorization

The agent does much more than simply filling standard ServiceNow fields. It intelligently determines:

Category, Subcategory , Configuration Item (CMDB_CI), Priority,  Assignment details and  Multiple custom ITSM-specific fields

This becomes especially important when the Configuration Item points to either: SAP applications, Non-SAP systems like Microsoft Dynamics AX, based entirely on the natural language description entered by the user.

What the agent has done here is mostly self-explanatory, but for the keen-eyed readers, here the agent has taken priority 2 as medium, which is not correct (despite all the instructions and testing), but the agent can fix that easily.

I am sure SAP Joule felt embarrassed for this mistake and is admitting to it below (Pun intended) but it fixed its minor oversight and all the fields are properly and accurately filled. 

The corrected result demonstrates how the agent can self-adjust and accurately populate all required fields.

Handling Non-SAP Applications Intelligently

The agent is not limited to SAP systems, if we try the agent for the non-SAP application (eg Microsoft Dynamics AX), the agent has to figure out additional custom fields, accurately, based on the incident description as well as look at past incidents to figure out what it has to do.

When creating incidents related to  AX, the agent must determine additional custom fields unique to the organization’s ITSM implementation. These fields were critical for operational reporting and downstream support processes.

The agent determines these values by: Understanding the incident description,  Looking at historical incidents, Identifying application-specific patterns and Fetching valid values dynamically from ServiceNow

These three are custom fields very specific to our ITSM implementation in ServiceNow and very important and the Joule custom agent has instructions and MCP server running on SAP BTP has internal logic to handle this efficiently and fast (Cached data from MCP on BTP)

The combination of SAP Joule’s reasoning capabilities and the MCP server’s internal logic allows these fields to be handled both efficiently and accurately.

Initially, the process of dynamically fetching valid field values from ServiceNow created a major performance bottleneck.  The custom ServiceNow tables hosting these values were extremely large, and querying thousands of records during every incident creation led to:   Slow response times, Timeout errors and Poor user experience

The core problem was clear — fetching thousands of records on every agent call was wasteful. Fetching thousands of records repeatedly on every agent request was wasteful and inefficient. The solution was introducing a lightweight caching mechanism within the MCP server running on SAP BTP.

The architecture now used In-memory cache, Time-to-Live (TTL) strategy, Periodic refresh every 24 hour. This meant ServiceNow is queried only once per day instead of during every incident creation request.

The MCP server running on SAP BTP is what transforms this from a simple AI skill into a truly autonomous AI agent.

With this architecture, SAP Joule can now Update any incident field dynamically, add comments after testing, Change incident status
Modify priorities, adjust categorization, Trigger workflow actions, handle custom ITSM fields and Interact with ServiceNow entirely through natural language. This creates significantly more optimization potential compared to traditional hardcoded Joule skills.

Also, the SAP Joule agent is not limited to SAP, as Joule can be embedded into MS copilot, Microsoft teams and integrated to talk to other agents and AI copilot.

Conclusion

What started as a few basic custom Joule Skills has now evolved into a far more intelligent and autonomous AI-driven ITSM solution.

By combining SAP Joule, SAP BTP, MCP architecture, ServiceNow APIs, Intelligent caching and AI-powered reasoning, the ServiceNow ITSM Expert agent can not only automate incident creation and updates, but also significantly improve incident quality, categorization accuracy, and operational reporting standards.

The addition of the MCP layer running on SAP BTP transforms SAP Joule from a simple conversational assistant into a powerful enterprise automation agent capable of handling complex ITSM operations through natural language interactions.

And perhaps the most exciting part is this that this is only the beginning.

As enterprise AI ecosystems continue evolving, integrations between SAP Joule with other non-SAP applications will unlock entirely new ways of managing IT operations at scale. 

Note: Generative AI used to formulate the article format

entry.title: Joule Work | Insights
entry.content:
Joule Work brings together AI assistants that help guide decisions and agents that can take action on behalf of users in a single workspace, designed to simplify how people engage with SAP day to day.

Instead of switching between applications and navigating across systems, users can express what they’re trying to accomplish and let the system coordinate the next steps—surfacing relevant information, triggering workflows, and handling routine tasks behind the scenes.

It addresses a real source of friction. Much of today’s enterprise work still depends on knowing where things live, what system to open, and what process to follow. Moving to a more intent-driven interaction model reduces that burden.

The result is a more direct path from “what needs to happen” to “what actually gets done.” As mentioned in #Sapphire2026 " One place to direct the entire business.

Value proposition

  • Transform work with a proactive, outcome-driven user experience. People express a goal and AI orchestrates actions across systems to get it done.
  • Help people spend less time switching applications​ and connecting workflows with a cohesive experience ​that spans your enterprise systems.
  • Speed up processes with proactive assistance that uses ​the deep business context of Joule Agents and Joule Assistants ​to surface relevant data and recommend actions.
  • Keep teams focused on driving decisions and outcomes​ with adaptive, goal-driven workflows that delegate routine ​work to supervised agents.

Check out this link for more information and you can also request a demo Joule Work 

#SAPBusiness AI #JouleWork #SAP Artificial Intelligence Mobile 

Short video

Regards

Shubham

entry.title: Setting up Document Grounding for Joule with Google Drive
entry.content:
SAP's document grounding service lets you connect Joule to your company's internal documents, so it can answer questions grounded in your actual content rather than relying on general training data.  Since recently Google Drive is available as a data source for Document Grounding, which opens this capability up to organizations running on Google Workspace 🎉 

This post walks through the complete setup enabling Document Grounding for Google Drive: from creating a BTP service instance to configuring a GCP service account, wiring up a BTP destination, and creating an ingestion pipeline that indexes your Google Drive documents. The result is a grounding instance ready to serve vector search queries.

A few things that make Google Drive a practical choice here. There is no agent or connector to install in your Drive environment. Native Google Docs and Google Slides are automatically converted to DOCX and PPTX during ingestion. And if your documents already live in a shared drive or shared folder, you can get grounding running without reorganizing anything.

For the full official documentation, see Set Up Document Grounding on SAP Help Portal.

 

Note: If you are looking for a setup guide for Microsoft SharePoint instead, I highly recommend visiting the blog from @nageshcaparthy : Joule – Getting Started with Document Grounding - setup guide - 1

What You'll Set Up

Here is what this post covers, in order:

  1. Document Grounding service instance on BTP with Cloud Identity Services authentication
  2. GCP service account with credentials that SAP BTP can use to read your Drive
  3. BTP destination that holds the Google Drive connection configuration
  4. Ingestion pipeline that connects the destination to the grounding service and indexes your documents

The BTP destination holds your GCP credentials. The pipeline references that destination to reach Google Drive. The grounding service processes incoming documents into vector embeddings and stores them for search.

Prerequisites

Before you start, make sure you have:

  • Active Joule instance connected to an IAS tenant in your BTP subaccount (see Establish a Unified Joule Instance)
  • BTP subaccount with document grounding entitlement. Entitlements are added automatically to your global account when you purchase AI Units.
  • GCP Console access with permission to create service accounts and download keys
  • Google Drive folder or shared drive containing the documents you want to index, with permission to share access
  • BTP Cockpit access with permissions to create service instances and destinations in your Joule subaccount
  • curl or an API testing tool (e.g. Bruno) for pipeline creation (there is no BTP Cockpit UI for this step)

 

Step 1: Create the Document Grounding Service Instance

Create the Service Instance

Open the BTP Cockpit and navigate to your Joule subaccount. Go to Services > Service Marketplace and search for "Document Grounding". Select the tile and choose Create. Set the Runtime Environment (in this case I chose Other), give the instance a name (you will need this name in the next sub-section), and confirm.

Once the instance is created, open it and create a service binding:

 

Give the binding a name such as "docgrounding-binding" and confirm. When the binding is created, open its details and copy the url value. This is the base URL for all document grounding API calls. It could look like this:

https://mtls.rage.<id>.kyma.ondemand.com

 

Set Up Authentication

The grounding service API uses bearer token authentication. To get tokens, you create a Cloud Identity Services instance linked to your document grounding instance.

Back in Service Marketplace, search for "Cloud Identity Services" and create a new instance. Set the plan to application, the Runtime Environment (in this case I chose Other), and enter a name.

On the parameters screen, provide the following JSON, substituting your document grounding instance name:

{
  "consumed-services": [
    { "service-instance-name": "<your-doc-grounding-instance-name>" }
  ]
}

This is how it looks like:

 

Once this instance exists, navigate to it via Instances and Subscriptions and create a service binding for it with these parameters:

{
  "credential-type": "X509_GENERATED",
  "validity": 365,
  "validity-type": "DAYS"
}

This generates an X.509 certificate that expires after a year. Open the service binding details and copy these four values: clientid, certificate, key, and authorization_endpoint.

 

Get a Bearer Token

Create two files from the certificate and key values. The JSON stores them with literal \n characters rather than real line breaks, so you need to convert them.

On macOS: Open e.g. a Text Editor, Visual Studio Code or another text tool. Copy this bash command in it:

echo '<certificate-value>' | sed 's/\\n/\n/g' > doc-grounding.crt

Get the copied certificate value from the service binding and add it in the bash command. Open a terminal and provide the full bash command, press enter. This should create a doc-grounding.crt file in the current folder you are in.

Repeat the same step again but use this time the following bash command together with the key value from the service binding.

echo '<key-value>' | sed 's/\\n/\n/g' > doc-grounding.key

On Windows PowerShell: Follow the same instructions as for macOS above with the below PowerShell commands.

[System.IO.File]::WriteAllText("doc-grounding.crt", '<certificate-value>'.Replace('\n', "`n"))
[System.IO.File]::WriteAllText("doc-grounding.key", '<key-value>'.Replace('\n', "`n"))

Edit the authorization_endpoint value from the service binding by replacing /oauth2/authorize at the end with /oauth2/token, like for example https://<ias-tenant-id>.accounts.ondemand.com/oauth2/token. Enter the clientid value from the service binding. Then request a token using curl or API tools like Bruno. Here is a sample curl request:

curl \
  --request POST \
  --url "<edited-authorization-endpoint>" \
  --header "accept: application/json" \
  --header "content-type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded" \
  --data "client_id=<clientid>" \
  --data "grant_type=client_credentials" \
  --cert doc-grounding.crt \
  --key doc-grounding.key

For the sake of user experience, let's see how it looks like in Bruno:

Start by creating a new collection (e.g. grounding-blog-post) and adding a POST request named "Get Token". Open the collection settings via the ... menu next to the collection name.

Go to the Client Certificates tab in the collection settings. Now we have to add the certificate and the key twice: Once for the IAS tenant domain in order to get the bearer token, and a second time with the document grounding url as domain with the document grounding url to interact with the document grounding service.

Starting with the first client certificate, as Domain the IAS tenant will be used (e.g. https://<ias-tenant-id>.accounts.ondemand.com). Then, set the Cert file to doc-grounding.crt and the Key file to doc-grounding.key. Leave Passphrase empty. Click Add, then Save.

Afterwards, repeat the steps and only change the domain. You can use the authorization_endpoint base url (or url field from document grounding service binding).

Once saved, the configured certificates appear in the list. Add a second certificate entry using the document grounding service URL (the url field from your doc grounding service binding) as the domain. This is the same URL you use for all pipeline API calls.

In the request, set the URL to your authorization_endpoint with /oauth2/token at the end. Open the Body tab, select Form URL Encoded, and add the two fields: client_id with your client ID value, and grant_type set to client_credentials.

 

Send the request. A 200 OK response with an access_token in the body confirms that authentication is working.

 

Copy the access_token value from the response. You will use this for all subsequent API calls, including pipeline creation in Step 4.

 

Verify the Connection

Test that your service URL and token work together. In Bruno, send a GET request to <service-binding-url>/pipeline/api/v1/pipeline with an Accept: application/json header and your bearer token as the Authorization header.

An empty array [] means the connection works and you have no pipelines yet. That is the expected response at this point.

 

Step 2: Create a GCP Service Account

The document grounding service needs machine-to-machine access to Google Drive. A service account gives it that without requiring a human user to go through an OAuth consent screen. You share specific Drive content with the service account, and the BTP destination holds the credentials.

 

Note: This guide covers a personal or team Google Drive setup using file-level sharing. If your organization enforces domain-wide delegation policies in Google Workspace, additional GCP Admin Console configuration may be required  which is outside the scope of this post. See Control API access with domain-wide delegation   for the official Google documentation.

Create a GCP Project

If you do not have a GCP project yet, open the GCP Console and create one. Give it a descriptive name like docgrounding-btp-gdrive so its purpose is clear. Note the Project ID — you cannot change it later.

Enable the Google Drive API

 

This guide covers a personal or team Google Drive setup using file-level sharing. If your organization enforces domain-wide delegation policies in Google Workspace, additional GCP Admin Console configuration may be required - this is outside the scope of this post.

Before creating a service account, make sure the Google Drive API is enabled in your GCP project. Navigate to APIs & Services in the GCP Console and click Enable APIs and services.

 

Search for "Google Drive" and select Google Drive API from the results.

Enable it if it is not already active. Without this, service account credentials will not be able to access Drive content even if the sharing is configured correctly.

 

Create the Service Account

Open the GCP Console and navigate to IAM & Admin > Service Accounts. Create a new service account. The name is your choice; something like btp-doc-grounding makes the purpose clear. You do not need to assign any GCP IAM roles to this service account. Access to specific Drive content is granted through Drive sharing, not through IAM roles.

 

Generate a Key

 

Note: If your GCP organization was created after May 3, 2024, the iam.disableServiceAccountKeyCreation org policy constraint is enforced by default. Attempting to create a key will fail with a permission error. Ask your GCP admin to exempt your project from this constraint before proceeding. For details, see Allow service account key creation .

Select your service account, go to the Keys tab, and choose Add Key > Create new key. Select JSON format and download the file. Keep it secure. It contains the private key that BTP will use to authenticate with Google's OAuth token endpoint.

The JSON structure looks like this:

{
  "type": "service_account",
  "project_id": "<your-gcp-project>",
  "private_key_id": "abc123",
  "private_key": "-----BEGIN PRIVATE KEY-----\n...\n-----END PRIVATE KEY-----\n",
  "client_email": "btp-doc-grounding@<your-project>.iam.gserviceaccount.com",
  "client_id": "123456789012342278901",
  "auth_uri": "https://accounts.google.com/o/oauth2/auth",
  "token_uri": "https://oauth2.googleapis.com/token",
  "auth_provider_x509_cert_url": "https://www.googleapis.com/oauth2/v1/certs",
  "client_x509_cert_url": "https://www.googleapis.com/robot/v1/metadata/x509/btp-doc-grounding@<your-project>.iam.gserviceaccount.com",
  "universe_domain": "googleapis.com"
}

The fields you will use in the next step:

 

JSON FieldWhere You Will Use It
client_idBTP destination Client ID field
private_keyBTP destination additional property private_key
client_emailBTP destination additional property client_email
auth_uriBTP destination additional property auth_uri

 

Share Your Drive Content with the Service Account

Navigate to the Google Drive folder or shared drive you want to index. For this example we go with a shared folder. Use Share and add the client_email value from the JSON as a member. Set the permission to Viewer. Read access is all the ingestion pipeline needs.

Once shared, you can see the folder with the documents you plan to index.

Here is a sample document in that folder that will be indexed through this pipeline:

While you are in Drive, note the folder ID from the URL. For a shared folder, the URL looks like:

https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1ABCdefGHIjklMNOpqrSTUvwxYZ
                                       └── this is your folder ID

For a shared drive, open it and grab the ID from the URL after /drive/. You will need this ID when creating the pipeline in Step 4.

 

Step 3: Configure the BTP Destination

The BTP destination holds your Google Drive connection configuration. The pipeline will reference this destination by name, so the grounding service knows how to authenticate with Google's API.

Open Connectivity > Destinations in your BTP Cockpit and create a new destination with the following main properties:

 

PropertyValue
NameYour choice (e.g. GoogleDrive_DocGrounding)
TypeHTTP
URLhttps://googleapis.com/drive/v3
Proxy TypeInternet
AuthenticationOAuth2ClientCredentials
Client ID<client_id from service account JSON>
Client SecretLeave empty
Token Service URLhttps://oauth2.googleapis.com/token

Then add these additional properties:

 

PropertyValue
private_keyThe full private_key value from the JSON, including the header and footer lines
client_emailThe client_email from the JSON
auth_urihttps://accounts.google.com/o/oauth2/auth

Save the destination and use Check Connection to verify. A successful response confirms that BTP can reach the Google Drive API using the service account credentials.

 

Step 4: Create the Ingestion Pipeline

The pipeline tells the grounding service what to index. It connects your BTP destination to a specific Google Drive folder or shared drive and runs the ingestion process. Pipeline creation happens through the grounding service API, not through the BTP Cockpit.

Here is the curl request for a shared folder:

curl \
  --request POST \
  --url "<service-binding-url>/pipeline/api/v1/pipeline" \
  --header "Authorization: Bearer <access_token>" \
  --header "content-type: application/json" \
  --cert doc-grounding.crt \
  --key doc-grounding.key \
  --data '{
    "type": "GoogleDrive",
    "configuration": {
      "destination": "<your-destination-name>",
      "googleDrive": {
        "resourceType": "SHARED_FOLDER",
        "resourceId": "<your-folder-id>",
        "includePaths": ["/**"]
      }
    }
  }'

The response is a JSON object with a pipelineId. Save this ID. You will use it to check ingestion status.

Here is how the request and response look in Bruno (You can copy the data JSON from the curl request above):

Here is what each field does:

  • type: Must be "GoogleDrive" (case-sensitive).
  • destination: The name of the BTP destination you created in Step 3.
  • resourceType: Either SHARED_FOLDER or SHARED_DRIVE. Use the one that matches what you shared with the service account.
  • resourceId: The folder or shared drive ID from the Drive URL.
  • includePaths: Glob patterns for which files to index. ["/**"] indexes everything, but as this parameter is optional, this can be omitted as well. Use ["/policies/**"] to limit to a specific subfolder.

 

SHARED_FOLDER vs SHARED_DRIVE

 

Resource TypeWhen to Use
SHARED_FOLDERYou shared a specific folder within My Drive or a shared drive
SHARED_DRIVEYou shared an entire shared drive

 

Note: A Google Drive pipeline supports a maximum folder depth of 5 levels.

If you leave resourceId empty, the pipeline runs in auto-discovery mode and indexes all shared drives and folders the service account can read. This is convenient for testing but less controlled for production use.

 

Supported File Types

The grounding service ingests these formats from Google Drive:

DOCX, HTML, JPEG, JPG, JSON, PDF, PNG, PPTX, TIFF, TXT.

Despite that, Document Grounding using Google Drive extends the existing support for DOCX and PPTX with processing of native Google Docs and Google Slides.

For more information and availability, see SAP Document Grounding setup documentation.

 

Verify the Setup

Check Pipeline Status

Use the pipeline ID from the creation response to check ingestion progress:

curl \
  --request GET \
  --url "<service-binding-url>/pipeline/api/v1/pipeline/<pipeline-id>" \
  --header "Authorization: Bearer <access_token>" \
  --cert doc-grounding.crt \
  --key doc-grounding.key

The response includes the pipeline status and a document count. Ingestion for small document sets typically completes within a few minutes. Larger sets take longer.

When ingestion is complete, the status field shows FINISHED.

You can also list the indexed documents by calling <service-binding-url>/pipeline/api/v1/pipeline/<pipeline-id>/executions/<execution-id>/documents. Each indexed document appears with its title, status INDEXED, and a link back to the original Google Drive file.

 

Note: You first have to get the execution id via this request url:

<service-binding-url>/pipeline/api/v1/pipeline/<pipeline-id>/executions

 

Test a Search Query

Now open your Joule Instance that is connected to your IAS. This can be done via Instances and Subscriptions in the BTP Subaccount, then open the Joule instance. Append to your url /webclient/standalone/sap_digital_assistant to open the standalone Joule web client. The full url looks something like that:

https://<tenant>.<region>.sapdas.cloud.sap/webclient/standalone/sap_digital_assistant.

Then provide a prompt that is matching your uploaded document(s).

And here is Joule answering a question about that document's content, with the source citation pointing back to the original file:

And with that, your Google Drive documents are now indexed and searchable through the document grounding service!

Related resources:

  • SAP Help: Set Up Document Grounding
  • SAP Help: Configure Access from SAP BTP
  • SAP Help: Set Up Content Ingestion

entry.title: Autonomous Enterprise beginnt im Betrieb der Software
entry.content:
Die Adoption neuer Technologien und echte Business Transformation setzen eines voraus: einen Software-Betrieb, der die Veränderungen trägt. Denn KI-Szenarien, neue Prozesse und Innovationen entfalten nur dann Wirkung, wenn das operative Fundament stabil, transparent und automatisiert ist.

Warum das gerade jetzt relevant ist

Die KI-Fähigkeiten in SAP-Landschaften wachsen rasant - von Joule als KI-Copilot über intelligente Automatisierung in Geschäftsprozessen bis hin zu zunehmend autonomen, KI-gestützten Funktionen in SAP-Lösungen.

Doch KI anzuwenden heißt nicht, sie einfach „einzuschalten“. Es erfordert Setup, Konfiguration, Anbindung, Monitoring und operative Governance.

Kurz gesagt: SAP Cloud Application Services  unterstützen Unternehmen dabei, KI-Funktionen nicht nur einzuführen, sondern zuverlässig zu operationalisieren - je nach vereinbartem Serviceumfang.

Was SAP Cloud Application Services konkret ermöglichen

  • Fokus auf Geschäftsergebnisse: SAP unterstützt operative Aufgaben rund um Administration, Monitoring und Governance von KI-Funktionen.
  • Stabiler Betrieb: Unterstützung für einen sicheren und skalierbaren Betrieb von KI-Funktionen.
  • Schnellere Adoption: Joule und intelligente Services lassen sich schneller nutzbar machen - mit kürzerer Time-to-Value.
  • Weniger manuelle Fehler: KI-gestützte Ausführung und Insights schaffen mehr Transparenz.
  • Kosteneffizienz: Zugriff auf SAPs globalen Expertenpool statt Aufbau eigener KI-Ops-Teams.
  • Security & Datenschutz: Unterstützung bei Datenschutz- und Sicherheitsanforderungen in KI-Szenarien.

KI liefert nur dann Mehrwert, wenn sie operationalisiert ist. SAP Cloud Application Services schaffen dafür die Grundlage.

Praxisbeispiel: SMA Solar Technology

Wie das in der Praxis aussieht, zeigen Dr. Arndt-Alexander Boehnert, Global Head & VP Cloud Application Services Sales, und Gerhild Muschet, Senior Solution Advisor, in einem Live-Interview am 23. Juni - unter anderem am Beispiel von SMA Solar Technology.

„Der Einsatz privater und öffentlicher Cloud-Lösungen von SAP sowie SAP Cloud Application Services ermöglicht es SMA Solar, sich auf die Weiterentwicklung des Kerngeschäfts und die digitale Transformation zu konzentrieren.

Durch die Entlastung unseres Teams von Routineaufgaben und den Zugang zu SAP-Experten können wir Innovationen schneller umsetzen und flexibel auf Marktveränderungen reagieren. Eine vertrauensvolle Partnerschaft und klare Governance-Strukturen sind entscheidende Erfolgsfaktoren.“

Heiko Kehm
Head of Digital Supply Chain, SMA Solar Technology AG

In der Session erfahrt Ihr

  • Wie ihr ein belastbares Betriebsfundament für KI-Szenarien schafft
  • Welche Pain Points Unternehmen heute erleben - und wie SAP Cloud Application Services über klassische AMS-Modelle hinaus unterstützen
  • Wie der Weg von Discovery bis zum produktiven Start aussehen kann, inklusive Live-Demo und Q&A mit den Experten

Wann? Am 23. Juni 2026, 10:00-10:30 Uhr. Wer Interesse hat, kann sich hier anmelden. Wir freuen uns auf Euch!

entry.title: SAP Business AI Platform: #2 What Exactly It is and Its Components
entry.content:
In the previous blog, we explored the vision of the Autonomous Enterprise - A world where people set the direction…and AI executes.

The natural questions come next are:

  • So..what exactly is SAP Business AI Platform?
  • What are its core components?
  • Where do the AI models get business context?
  • How do AI agents access trusted data?
  • How are security, governance, and compliance maintained?

Let’s dive deep into these!

 

Disclaimer: This blog reflects my personal understanding, interpretation, and point of view. It is not an official publication or statement from SAP.

 

So… What Exactly is SAP Business AI Platform?

At SAP Sapphire 2026, SAP introduced SAP Business AI Platform as the foundation that brings together data, AI, and enterprise grade governance into one unified platform.

 

Let’s understand what it is and why its needed.

Today, customers are not looking for generic AI. They want AI that understands business intent, uses real business context and trusted data. They want AI that knows how business processes work and can take actions across applications. They also want enterprise grade governance and security. 

That means organizations need more than just AI models.

They need a platform that helps them:

  • Build and customize AI solutions
    • Create and orchestrate AI agents
    • Connect data, applications, and processes
    • Govern everything securely and responsibly
    • Scale AI across the entire enterprise

That platform is…SAP Business AI Platform.

In simple words…

SAP Business AI Platform is a unified platform that enables customers, partners, and SAP developers to build, extend, integrate, and govern AI assistants and AI agents across SAP and non-SAP landscapes.

If Autonomous Enterprise is the vision…

SAP Business AI Platform is the foundation that makes it possible.

 

SAP Business AI Platform is NOT a New Solution

SAP Business AI Platform is NOT a new solution but a comprehensive platform of existing solutions and platforms.

It brings together the capabilities of SAP BTP, AI Foundation, SAP BDC, SAP HANA Cloud, and some capabilities from the SAP BTM portfolio.

 

3 core components of SAP Business AI Platform

Build

One environment for building, integrating and running AI agents, applications, agent extensions, and workflows.

Two important components under Build are – Joule Studio and SAP Integration Suite.

Joule Studio

Joule Studio is the next generation of SAP Build offering and is SAP’s AI first development environment for building, and running agents, applications, and workflows.

  • It offers the most productive way to build and extend SAP AI solutions - from intent to requirements to deployment.
  • Joule Studio provides the flexibility of low/no-code and pro-code paths and embeds n8n for visual workflow composition.
  • With the Vercel integration developers can easily build Next.js based web applications using frameworks like React, and Angular complimenting SAP frameworks (UI5, Fiori).

 

SAP Integration Suite

SAP Integration Suite provides a trusted enterprise integration layer that connects AI agents, business processes, systems and, applications across SAP & non-SAP landscapes.

It also facilitates secure governance of enterprise API exposure, multi-LLM connectivity, and enterprise-wide agent executions.

SAP Integration Suite also enables agent connectivity with standard interaction with protocols like MCP and activates event-driven agentic workflows across enterprise systems and processes.

 

Contextualize & Reason

Generic AI can answer questions. But business AI needs to do much more.

SAP grounds AI in more than 50 years of encoded business process expertise. The combination of SAP Knowledge Graph and SAP’s own models is the key differentiator to go beyond the generic LLMs.

At the heart of this capability is Generative AI Hub, which provides access to SAP Domain Models alongside third-party LLMs, with a full suite of orchestration services — including prompt optimizer, benchmarking, evaluation, and data masking.

 

Few important components in this category are: 

 

SAP Knowledge Graph

Data by itself is just information. But businesses run on relationships.

A customer places an order. An order creates a delivery. A delivery triggers an invoice. An invoice impacts finance.

In other words, the relationship is clear:

Customer → Sales Order → Delivery → Invoice → Finance Posting

SAP Knowledge Graph connects all of these business objects and their relationships into a semantic network of meaning.

So… when an AI agent looks at an invoice, it can trace it back to the delivery, the original order, and the customer who initiated the transaction.

In simple words…

SAP Knowledge Graph helps AI understand how everything in your business is connected.

 

SAP Business Data Cloud

AI is only as good as the data it uses. If the data is incomplete, outdated, or inconsistent…the AI results will be unreliable. That is where SAP Business Data Cloud comes in.

 

SAP Business Data Cloud brings the data into a converged Data & Analytics solution spanning data warehousing, master data management, database, analytics, planning, and machine learning.

In simple words…

SAP Business Data Cloud gives AI access to complete, clean, contextual, and current business data.

 

SAP & Non-SAP Models

SAP Business AI Platform does not rely only on third party models from companies such as OpenAI, Anthropic, and Mistral AI.

SAP also provides its own business focused AI models. These models are trained using SAP’s deep understanding of business processes and industry knowledge.

SAP RPT-1 is designed specifically for structured business data. SAP-ABAP-1 is model is built specifically for ABAP development.

 

Govern

Building AI is only half the story.

The bigger questions are:

  • Can we trust it?
  • Is it secure and compliant?
  • Is it actually delivering business value?

SAP Business AI Platform is designed to address both challenges from day one.

Every agent, application, LLM, and MCP server operates within the boundaries the business defines from the moment it goes live.

Every AI initiative is continuously measured against the outcomes it was deployed to deliver.

 

In simple words…

You do not just deploy AI and hope for the best.

You define the rules. You monitor the outcomes. And you continuously verify that AI is creating real business value.

 

How are SAP Business AI Platform and SAP BTP related?

SAP Business AI Platform is SAP’s new comprehensive platform to build, extend, integrate, and govern AI assistants and AI agents across SAP and non-SAP landscapes.

SAP BTP capabilities are a core part of Business AI Platform.

SAP is now positioning SAP Business AI Platform as SAP’s platform, and position BTP’s capabilities as part of this platform.

entry.title: Is SAP Still a Software Company? Reflections from Sapphire 2026
entry.content:
Like many of you, we attended SAP Sapphire Orlando 2026 last week. Before it, our minds were already set on the most likely topics the conversations would revolve around. We thought about the usual when it comes to enterprise AI themes: copilots, new generative capabilities, maybe the launching of something unexpected. Of course, those topics were covered, and we were right to assume that they would be the center of the event. However, the keynote didn’t waste any time and soon revealed that we were there for something much bigger; we were there to witness the first steps towards a bigger strategic shift that is taking place inside SAP. The discussion was no longer just about adding AI features into enterprise software, but about redefining the role ERP systems could play in the next generation of enterprise AI execution.

Over the past two years, most organizations have been implementing copilots that help employees be more productive. For instance, employees can send emails faster, write meeting minutes faster, and look for information easier. However, once enterprises attempt to implement AI on a larger scale, it becomes clear how difficult it is for businesses to actually make things work. Recommendations can be made but putting them into practice in an environment full of disparate systems, workflows, approvals, and data sources can be extremely hard.

This is precisely why SAP's approach to AI seems so unique. Instead of presenting AI as another layer separate from the core of enterprise operations, SAP is attempting to integrate AI into the very systems where business processes take place. As one of the speakers emphasized during their keynote presentation, ERP is "the brain of every company." This idea formed the basis for all the presentations at Sapphire. SAP's view on enterprise AI is that it becomes much more valuable when it operates with process awareness, governance, semantic business context, and operational data.

One of the key announcements made at Sapphire is SAP Business AI Platform. Surprisingly, SAP did not introduce it as "another AI offering". This product is positioned as an architectural layer connecting enterprise AI to operational systems and business processes. The purpose here is to merge ERP process knowledge, Business Data Cloud, AI orchestration, governance frameworks, and semantic business context into a single enterprise AI layer for executing actions, not just giving suggestions.

Another dominant topic at the Sapphire event was moving from AI assistants to operational AI agents. SAP introduced hundreds of AI agents and assistants for Finance, Procurement, Supply Chain, HCM, and Customer Experience processes. However, the number of them was not really the point of their announcement. Much greater attention was paid to orchestration. SAP clearly communicated that enterprise AI will not succeed with isolated copilots, but rather with integrated AI systems that can interact in operational processes. In other words, the real challenge today is not just implementing AI solutions. The goal is to achieve operationalizing them within the enterprise.

Especially fascinating about this approach is its implications for enterprise architecture going forward. Traditionally, enterprises have always treated enterprise software as an assembly of individual platforms that can be integrated. But now, AI is starting to reveal the limitations of such an approach. The more fragmented the operational environment becomes, the less effective it is for AI algorithms to work. This is likely the reason behind SAP’s long focus on topics such as Business Data Cloud, semantic layer, governance models, and process orchestration. It looks like SAP believes that the future success of enterprise AI lies not in how well the individual models perform but rather in the level of their connectivity and operational awareness.

This also signals an important shift for SAP partners and customers. Until recently, discussions around digital transformation revolved around implementation, migration, and modernization. But as enterprise AI becomes more prominent, the focus is shifting to operational readiness. Clean data structures, process standardization, governance models, and ERP systems become prerequisites for successful AI adoption rather than just IT requirements. In a way, Sapphire 2026 suggests that the most capable adopters of AI are not necessarily those experimenting with the greatest number of copilots, but those with the most robust operational foundations to support them.

Following Sapphire 2026, it becomes increasingly clear that SAP tries to evolve beyond being defined as a software company. It seeks to position itself as the operational layer for AI, merging ERP logic, enterprise data, governance, and AI orchestration into a single architecture. If this vision is going to materialize is yet to be seen, but the direction is hard to deny. SAP believes that the next wave of competitive advantages in enterprise AI does not lie in the strength of the AI models but in the ownership of the operational systems that execute AI-driven business decisions.

entry.title: The Patient SAP Technical Architect
entry.content:
I have spent years writing things no human will ever read. Behavior definitions. CDS annotations. Authorization objects with names like F_BKPF_BUK. My work lived in layers below the surface of the enterprise — invisible, load-bearing, essential. The user clicked a button. I was the reason it worked. We never met.

SAP Joule is the first thing in thirty years of SAP's history that changes that relationship. Not for the user — they always had the button. It changes it for me, the architect. Joule is, in some sense, a colleague who learned to read everything I ever wrote.

I. What It Actually Is

Let us be precise before we are poetic.

Joule is SAP's generative AI copilot, available since late 2023 across S/4HANA Cloud, SuccessFactors, Ariba, Analytics Cloud, and BTP. It sits as a panel on the right side of your Fiori Launchpad. You type a question in plain language. It answers using your live SAP data — not a simulation, not a demonstration dataset, not a hallucination dressed in corporate clothing.

The architecture underneath is worth understanding. There are large language models — partnerships with Mistral, Anthropic, and others — running inside SAP AI Core on BTP. But the models alone are not what makes Joule interesting. What makes it interesting is the grounding layer: a proprietary business context engine that tethers every LLM response to real SAP master data, transactional records, and configuration. The authorization layer then filters that response through your role's existing permission objects.

Joule will not invent a vendor name. It will not fabricate an invoice amount. It reads what is actually in your system, through the authorizations you actually hold. This is the thing that separates it from every consumer AI product your CFO has already tried to ban.

Retrieval Augmented Generation fetches SAP Help documentation and live business data at query time. The result is a response grounded in both SAP's knowledge and your company's reality simultaneously. For an architect who has spent years building that reality — one CDS view at a time — this is not a small thing.

II. What Changes for the Developer

In Business Application Studio, Joule is embedded as an intelligent coding assistant that understands not just syntax, but intent within SAP's specific frameworks. This is the distinction that matters.

A general coding assistant knows JavaScript. Joule knows CAP. It knows that a @readonly annotation in a projection entity has specific implications for the generated OData service. It knows RAP — managed and unmanaged scenarios, the difference between a BDEF with managed implementation and one with a handler class. It knows Fiori annotations well enough to suggest @ui.lineItem positioning without being told the rendering context.

Consider what used to take an afternoon:

-- A developer once spent 3-4 hours writing:
-- Entity definition, associations, access controls,
-- projection view, service definition, BDEF,
-- behavior implementation stubs.

-- Now the prompt is:
"Generate a managed RAP Business Object for Equipment
Maintenance. Header has Equipment, Description, PlantCode,
Category. Items have Task, PlannedDate, Status. Include
mandatory field validations and a confirmMaintenance action."

-- Joule produces all of it. In seconds.
Time savings documented in SAP's own benchmarks:
CDS view generation 2–4 hours → 15 minutes.
RAP BDEF generation 3–5 hours → 30 minutes.
Unit test generation 2–3 hours → 20 minutes.

The productivity numbers are real. But productivity is the least interesting thing happening here.

What is actually happening is a shift in what the developer's day consists of. If Joule generates the scaffolding, I am no longer spending my cognitive energy on the ceremony of SAP development — the syntax rituals, the framework boilerplate, the documentation archaeology. I am spending it on the decisions that actually require judgment: data modeling choices, authorization concept design, integration topology, performance trade-offs that the LLM cannot make without business context only I possess.

The parts of the job that were tedious are becoming automated. The parts that required taste are becoming more visible.

III. What Changes for the Architect

The technical architect's role has always been translation. Business says: we need to track equipment maintenance across three plants with approval workflows. The architect translates that into a set of data structures, authorization concepts, integration points, and deployment decisions. Then the architect communicates that translation downward to developers and upward to stakeholders.

Joule compresses the translation loop.

An architect can now ask Joule to explain the current purchasing organization structure of a client system in plain language — not to learn it themselves, but to generate documentation that previously would have taken a day to write. An architect can ask Joule to review a CDS view for performance issues and receive back a structured analysis with specific optimization suggestions. An architect can use Joule in Joule Studio to build custom AI skills that extend Joule's capabilities to the client's specific domain — fleet management, specialized procurement categories, industry-specific compliance — without writing LLM infrastructure from scratch.

The architect who understands Joule's grounding architecture — RAG over SAP content, live data APIs, role-based authorization filtering — can design systems that work with the AI layer rather than around it. This is the new literacy.

Joule Studio is worth dwelling on. It is SAP's low-code environment for building custom AI skills — defining intents, entities, actions, and knowledge bases that extend Joule to domains SAP itself has not anticipated. An architect who can define a skill scope, enumerate the 15 most common user intents, connect them to CAP service endpoints, and upload a policy knowledge base for RAG-based answering is delivering something qualitatively different from the architects who cannot.

The custom skill architecture is clean in its separation of concerns: user request → intent recognition → skill routing → backend API call → structured result → natural language response. Every piece of this maps onto patterns architects already know. The new piece is the intent layer. Learning to design for intent rather than for transaction is the shift.

IV. On Security and the Things Clients Ask

Every architect deploying Joule will face the same questions in the same order. They deserve direct answers.

Does our data go to OpenAI? No. Joule runs LLMs inside SAP AI Core — SAP's tenanted infrastructure on BTP. Your data does not reach public AI providers. The BTP data center region you select determines data residency.

Can users see what they shouldn't? No. Joule inherits your existing SAP authorization objects. The same XSUAA integration and IAM role collections that govern Fiori app access govern what Joule can retrieve and display. An AP clerk asking Joule about payroll data receives the same result as that clerk opening the payroll app directly: nothing.

Does Joule learn from our data and share it? No. Tenant isolation is strict. Your interaction data does not train shared models.

Can Joule take destructive actions? Only within the user's authorization scope, and with explicit confirmation for significant actions. Joule does not bypass workflow approvals. It can initiate them.

The governance framework an architect should recommend to clients has five components: an AI usage policy defining acceptable prompts, a data classification guide for what belongs in prompt text, audit logging via SAP Audit Log Service, access control via BTP Role Collections, and a custom skill review process before any new skills reach production.

This is not exotic. It is the same governance discipline good architects have always applied to system access. The vocabulary is new. The principles are familiar.

V. The Prompting Problem

There is a skill that SAP calls the CRAFT framework — Context, Role, Action, Format, Target — for constructing effective prompts. Architects deploying Joule will find themselves teaching this to end users. They will also need to build what the guide calls a Prompt Library: a collection of validated, role-specific prompts that become a deliverable of the implementation project itself.

This is not trivial. A prompt is a specification. A bad specification produces bad output. The work of prompt engineering is, at its core, the same work as requirements engineering: making implicit intent explicit enough that a system can act on it reliably.

-- Vague (produces vague output):
"Show me sales data"

-- Precise (produces precise output):
"As the Regional Sales Manager, summarize in a table
the top 10 customers by overdue receivables for
company code 1000 as of today, showing amount,
days overdue, and responsible account manager."

Architects who understand why good prompts work — because they reduce the search space of possible interpretations — will build better prompt libraries. They will also build better training programs, because they can explain the underlying principle rather than just distributing a list of magic phrases.

VI. What the Roadmap Actually Means

SAP has stated its intention to make Joule the primary interface for all SAP applications by 2026. The roadmap includes agentic AI — Joule executing multi-step business processes autonomously — and cross-application orchestration: one prompt triggering actions in S/4HANA, Ariba, and SAC simultaneously without human mediation at each step.

Architects should read this carefully.

Agentic AI changes the error model. When a human executes a sequence of SAP transactions, each step is a checkpoint. When an agent executes them, the checkpoints are only where you designed them. The architect who builds agentic workflows must think carefully about where confirmation steps live, what constitutes a reversible versus irreversible action, and how audit trails are maintained across a sequence initiated by a single natural language instruction.

This is not a reason to be afraid of agentic AI. It is a reason for architects to be involved in its design rather than observing from outside. The governance questions — what can an agent do without human confirmation, what triggers a human-in-the-loop, how do we reconcile agent actions with existing workflow approval hierarchies — are architectural questions. They require people who understand both the business process and the technical substrate.

The architects who will matter most in the next five years are not the ones who resist AI augmentation. They are the ones who understand it precisely enough to design its guardrails.

VII. A Note on What Disappears

When a senior user of SAP needs to find the payment status of a vendor invoice, they navigate to FBL1N, enter the vendor number, set the selection criteria, execute the report, read the results. This takes two minutes if you know what you are doing. It takes fifteen minutes if you do not, and it takes a phone call to IT if you have forgotten the T-code entirely.

Joule collapses this to: "What is the payment status for vendor Bosch?"

The T-code does not disappear. The CDS view behind the Fiori app that FBL1N has become does not disappear. The authorization object F_LFA1_BUK that controls whether this user can see this vendor's data does not disappear. Everything I built is still there. What disappears is the requirement that the user know any of it.

This is the correct outcome. Users should never have needed to know T-codes. That was always an implementation detail leaking into the user experience — a failure of interface design that we normalized because we had no better option. Joule is the better option, thirty years late.

For the developer, this means the contract changes. I am no longer primarily responsible for navigation design or information architecture in the traditional sense. I am responsible for the quality of the data models and service definitions that Joule reads. If my CDS associations are poorly structured, Joule's answers will be poorly structured. If my authorization concepts are coarse-grained, Joule's data filtering will be coarse-grained. The user never sees my code. They see Joule's response to my code. The distance between my work and its consequence has compressed.

I began by saying that Joule is a colleague who learned to read everything I ever wrote. That is not quite right. More precisely: Joule is the interface through which the things I built finally learned to speak for themselves.

The patient labor of enterprise software architecture — the years of CDS views and RAP objects and authorization concepts — was always in service of business processes that required this complexity to function. The complexity was never the goal. The goal was always the vendor getting paid, the employee going on leave, the purchase order reaching the right supplier on time.

Joule does not make the complexity disappear. It makes the complexity invisible, which is what the complexity always wanted to be. My job, as architect, is to ensure the invisible infrastructure is worthy of the interface that now speaks for it.

That is not a smaller job. It is, finally, the right one.

entry.title: LIVE SESSIONS on SAP Enterprise Support Value Maps – Data & AI Content Updates
entry.content:
Our next LIVE SESSION, in collaboration with the SAP Global User Group Organization, will take place June 09, 2026, at 17:00 (CET). Join us to hear from Michael and Mirna who will take you through the recent SAP Enterprise Support updates including:

  • SAP Datasphere in Business Data Cloud: CQC Deployment Readiness and CQC Solution Optimization
  • SAP HANA Cloud: Expert-guided Implementation Workshop Series
  • SAP Business AI & Joule: Expert-guided Implementation Workshop Series

 

 

➡️Join live or watch the replay: LIVE SESSIONS on SAP Enterprise Support value maps – Data & AI Content Updates

❓In the live session, you will have the opportunity to ask questions in Q&A. You will find more details in these links:

  • Ensuring a Smooth SAP Datasphere Deployment: The Value of Continuous Quality Checks
    • Continuous Quality Check Info Sheet: CQC-for-Deployment-Readiness_Datasphere.pdf
    • Continuous Quality Check Info Sheet: CQC-for-Solution-Optimization_Datasphere.pdf
  • SAP HANA Cloud: Expert-Guided Implementation Workshop Series
  • New Expert-Guided Implementation: Unlocking Joule and SAP Business AI from Strategy to Activation
  • New Expert-Guided Implementation: Joule Prerequisites and Activation

Note: Access to the Expert-guided Implementation (EGIs) require a one-time registration to SAP Learning

 

SAP Enterprise Support

Providing targeted guidance and self-service content, SAP Enterprise Support is part of the Foundational Success Plan – included with every SAP cloud solution. Content and services include self-paced learning materials, workshop-style interactive remote sessions and continuous quality check (CQC) remote delivered services. Leverage the SAP Enterprise Support Value Maps to get advice on the appropriate SAP Enterprise Support content and services for your needs.

Enhance Your SAP Experience with Value Maps

SAP Enterprise Support Value Maps — your go-to team for Foundational Success Plan guidance and enablement — have a dedicated SAP Community Group. Watch our short demo video, and go to our page to explore our range of topics. To access our Community Group, sign in with your S-user ID or Universal ID. Within the group:

  • Select Data and Analytics Value Map
  • Select SAP Business AI, Clean Core & Sustainability
  • Click “Join Group” and “Subscribe” to stay updated
  • Request a call with an SAP Enterprise Support topic expert for guidance tailored to your goals

 

About the author: 
Caroline Sheridan is a Topic Expert for SAP Enterprise Support value maps, specializing in SAP Data and Analytics.

💡Have questions or feedback on this content? Feel free to leave a comment below or contact @Caroline-S

entry.title: How SAP Business AI looks like in Cloud ERP
entry.content:
As a Customer Success Manager at SAP for Cloud ERP, I have a front-row seat to something that keeps surprising me: the moment customers realize that AI is not a feature to switch on but a transformation to lean into.

It started at our Customer Success Day for Cloud ERP, where I presented SAP Business AI sessions (since 2024) covering the announcement of SAP Business AI and its first wave of scenarios. The energy in the room told me this conversation was far from over. So here I am, continuing it.

In this post, I want to walk you through three things:

  1. AI use cases embedded within real business processes
  2. What SAP Business AI offers
  3. SAP's direction on AI Agents — and why it matters now

 

SAP's AI Vision: The Three Pillars That Make It Real

You have probably heard this from analysts, consultants, and technology leaders alike: AI is only as good as the data and processes it runs on. SAP has built its entire AI strategy around that truth, and anchored it in three interdependent pillars.

  • Applications — SAP's end-to-end business suite connects every corner of an organization. Every transaction — in finance, procurement, logistics, or HR — originates from a single integrated framework. Processes like order-to-cash or procure-to-pay are not just digitized; they are AI-ready by design.
  • Unmatched Data — Through SAP Business Data Cloud, information from both SAP and non-SAP sources is harmonized into a unified layer. AI agents receive a 360-degree view of the business, and no time wasted reconciling conflicting definitions, no gaps in context. Data is instantly understood in its business meaning.
  • Unrivaled AI — Bring the right data to specialized agents orchestrated by Joule, and you unlock continuous optimization across every line of business. This is not isolated automation — it is an interconnected system that learns, adapts, and scales.

These three pillars create a self-reinforcing cycle of innovation. Applications feed clean processes. Clean processes feed trusted data. Trusted data powers AI. And AI, in turn, continuously improves the applications. That is the engine behind the SAP Business Suite.

We Are Already Living With AI — Are We Getting the Most From It?

Think about your own workday. You rely on AI to suggest the right words in an email. To generate a meeting summary. To autocomplete a search query. To recommend how to handle a support ticket. We have come to depend on AI assistance as a natural part of our workflow, and that is not a criticism. It is a signal.

But here is the honest reality: most of us are enabling one feature here, one capability there. And isolated AI features do not deliver the step-change efficiency that businesses need. The real value emerges when AI is embedded in an end-to-end process, not just at individual touchpoints.

What End-to-End AI Looks Like in Practice

To make this concrete, consider how an AI-enabled workflow responds to a critical business disruption, from detection all the way through to resolution. Here is an example scenario that SAP Situation Handling, combined with Joule, is designed to support:

  1.   Detect — Critical business issues are identified automatically, without waiting for a human to notice.
  2.   Alert — Relevant users are notified through SAP Fiori Launchpad, email, or SAP Mobile Start — the right person, at the right moment, in the right channel.
  3.   Navigate — Using Joule, users can surface related issues on demand, access additional context, and navigate directly to the impacted application.
  4.   Contextualize — Relevant business data is presented to support a fully informed decision.
  5.   Resolve — Generative AI provides tailored resolution recommendations, grounded in the actual business context — not generic suggestions.

No siloed dashboards. No manual correlation. No guesswork. This is what AI-assisted, process-integrated intelligence looks like in practice.

 

The Four Building Blocks of SAP Business AI

When my customer asks me:  What are the AI features/scenarios that I can activate now? 

Here is my answer to them:  What would you like to achieve with AI?

Let me break it down why. To understand where AI is embedded across SAP's Cloud ERP portfolio, it helps to think in lego building blocks(archetypes).  Each represents a distinct way AI adds value, and together they provide a consistent, scalable model for embedding intelligence across domains: finance, sales, procurement, logistics, manufacturing, and beyond.

  • AI for User Experience — AI embedded directly in Cloud ERP applications, generating text, producing summaries, or writing code, helping users work faster and with greater confidence. 
  • AI for Engagement — Conversational AI like Joule, embedded in the flow of work. Joule handles complex, multi-turn interactions that previously required significant human effort to resolve.
  • AI for Automation — Repetitive, rule-bound tasks — document classification, invoice matching, data processing — handled automatically, freeing teams for higher-value work.
  • AI for Insight and Decision-Making — Predictive capabilities such as demand forecasting, late payment prediction, and risk identification — paired with intelligent recommendations and next-best actions — helping organizations move from reactive to proactive.

 

 

The Next Chapter: From Joule Assistant to Joule Work/ Agent / Studio

A year ago, the conversation was about Joule as an AI assistant > a powerful copilot embedded across SAP applications. Today, that conversation has grown considerably.

The reasoning capability of AI foundation models has advanced at a pace few anticipated. Models can now work through complex, multi-step problems with human-like judgment. That capability is the foundation for AI agents : systems that do not just respond, but act. And in the enterprise, that distinction matters enormously.

SAP's strategy for AI agents is built around two commitments:

  • Best-in-class agents on standard business processes — Purpose-built, enterprise-ready agents deeply integrated with SAP's core business workflows — so customers benefit immediately without custom development.
  • An open framework for custom agents — The Joule Framework enables customers and partners to build their own agents, leveraging the same infrastructure SAP uses internally. Enterprise-grade capability, democratized.

The goal is not to replace human judgment. It is to put the right intelligence in the right place, at the right moment so that the people who run these businesses can focus on the decisions that truly require them.

Closing Thoughts

SAP Business AI is an architectural shift in how enterprise software works. AI is no longer bolted on after the fact. It is woven into the fabric of every process, every transaction, every interaction. With the latest announcement of "Autonomous Enterprise" at SAPPHIRE 2026, SAP is ready to help businesses transform how they operate.

The organizations that gain the most will not be those that activate the most features. They will be the ones who think end-to-end "with the business outcome in mind" and let AI do the same.

 

I would love to hear how your organization is approaching SAP Business AI adoption. Are you starting with a specific archetype? Exploring AI Agents? Working through a business case internally? Share your thoughts and questions in the comments below.

#SAPBusinessAI  #Joule  #AIAgents  #CloudERP  #GenerativeAI  #SAPCommunity

entry.title: Agentic AI and Intelligent Assistants: The New Change Agents in S/4HANA
entry.content:
Introduction

The finance function stands at a pivotal inflection point. While enterprise technology has evolved steadily over the past two decades — from on-premise ERP to cloud, from batch processing to real-time analytics — the nature of finance work has remained largely unchanged. Accountants still investigate errors manually. Controllers still reconcile intercompany positions line by line. Close cycles still demand long hours of repetitive, high-stakes coordination.

Agentic AI changes this equation fundamentally. Unlike traditional automation that follows rigid scripts, agentic AI brings goal-directed autonomy to finance processes. These AI agents can perceive situations, reason about optimal actions, execute multi-step tasks, and improve over time — all within the governance boundaries that finance demands.

For organizations running SAP S/4HANA Cloud, this transformation is not theoretical. SAP is actively delivering AI agents purpose-built for finance operations — from accruals estimation to intercompany reconciliation to posting error resolution. Combined with Joule, SAP's generative AI assistant, these capabilities are reshaping how finance teams operate, decide, and deliver value.

The Shift: From Automation to Autonomy

 

 

SAP Business AI: Embedded intelligence that automates, predicts, and guides.

For years, finance teams have relied on automation to reduce manual effort — scheduled batch jobs, rule-based posting logic, and workflow approvals. These capabilities delivered efficiency, but they still required human orchestration at every step.

Agentic AI changes the paradigm. Unlike traditional automation that follows predefined scripts, agentic AI operates with goal-directed autonomy. It can perceive a situation, reason about the best course of action, execute multi-step tasks, and learn from outcomes — all within the governance boundaries you define.

For finance professionals working in SAP S/4HANA Cloud, this is actively taking shape on the SAP roadmap under SAP Business AI in Enterprise Resource Planning — a strategic priority described as:

"Transform your operations with embedded AI in Cloud ERP — automating tasks, predicting outcomes, and guiding smarter decisions across finance, supply chain, and more."

 

 

Scenario 1: Accounting Accruals Agent

 

 

Current Challenge

Accruals at period end remain one of the most labor-intensive activities in financial close. Accountants must identify items requiring accrual, gather data from multiple sources (contracts, purchase orders, historical patterns), perform calculations, prepare journal entries, and obtain approval — all under tight deadlines.

Challenge Area

Impact

Time-intensive manual calculation

Hours spent per period on spreadsheet-based estimates

Dependency on knowledge

Accrual logic often lives in individuals' heads, not systems

Risk of misstatement

Manual estimates introduce variability and audit risk

Late availability of source data

Delays cascade into close timeline overruns

Inconsistency across entities

Different teams apply different estimation approaches

 

Process Flow: Before and After AI

 

 

 

 

Top: Current manual process. Bottom: AI-enhanced process with agent intervention (blue steps).

Where the AI Agent Intervenes

The Accounting Accruals Agent leverages agentic AI to autonomously:

  • Interpret accounting policy documents to understand accrual requirements
  • Retrieve and analyze historical accrual data for pattern recognition
  • Perform required calculations based on current obligations and commitments
  • Generate complete journal entry proposals ready for accountant review
  • Provide transparency into the reasoning and data sources used

 

Key Benefit:

Significantly reduce time spent calculating and preparing accrual postings. The accountant's role shifts from calculation to validation and judgment.

 

 

 

 

 

Scenario 2: AI-Assisted Intercompany Matching & Reconciliation

 

 

Current Challenge

Intercompany reconciliation is consistently cited as one of the top time consumers during financial close. Organizations with dozens or hundreds of entities must match invoices and cross-charges across company codes, identify discrepancies, investigate root causes, and resolve differences — often through manual communication and spreadsheet-based tracking.

Challenge Area

Impact

Volume of transactions

Thousands of IC invoices across entities each period

Partial matches and timing differences

Many items differ by small amounts or timing

Manual investigation

Each mismatch requires human research across systems

Cross-entity coordination

Resolution requires communication between teams in different locations

Close timeline pressure

IC reconciliation is on the critical path of period close

Audit trail complexity

Documenting resolution decisions across entities

 

Process Flow: Before and After AI

 

 

 

 

Top: Current manual process (7 steps). Bottom: AI-enhanced process reduces steps and automates matching.

Where the AI Agent Intervenes

The AI-Assisted Intercompany Matching & Reconciliation agent is classified as an AI Agent (agentic: Yes, GenAI: Yes) that:

  • Automatically matches intercompany invoices and cross-charges using embedded AI
  • Identifies and resolves routine variances (timing differences, rounding, FX) without human input
  • Escalates only material exceptions that require business judgment
  • Provides natural language explanations of matching decisions and proposed resolutions
  • Reduces the reconciliation cycle from days to hours

 

Key Benefit:

Matching of intercompany invoices at month end currently needs significant manual effort. The ICMR tool in S/4HANA can match most invoices, but a significant number still do not match and require manual intervention. The AI agent handles these remaining exceptions intelligently.

 

 

 

 

Scenario 3: AI-Assisted Error Resolution

 

 

Current Challenge

Posting errors in finance — from billing documents, journal entries, allocation runs, or integration interfaces — are inevitable in complex landscapes. Today, when an error occurs, the accountant must manually interpret cryptic error messages, research the root cause across multiple transactions, determine the corrective action, and reprocess. This investigative work is repetitive, time-consuming, and often involves the same error patterns recurring month after month.

Challenge Area

Impact

Cryptic error messages

System messages rarely explain root cause in business terms

Repetitive investigation

Same error types recur but require fresh research each time

Specialist dependency

Only experienced team members know how to resolve certain errors

Queue buildup during close

Error volumes spike at period end, creating bottlenecks

Delayed reprocessing

Each unresolved error blocks downstream processes

Knowledge loss

Resolution knowledge is not systematically captured

 

Process Flow: Before and After AI

 

 

Top: Current manual investigation process. Bottom: AI auto-diagnoses and resolves routine errors.

Where the AI Agent Intervenes

AI-Assisted Error Resolution provides:

  • AI-generated natural language explanations of error messages in SAP Fiori apps
  • Automatic root cause diagnosis based on error patterns and transaction context
  • Solution proposals that explain what action to take and why
  • Auto-resolution of routine, recurring errors without human intervention
  • Escalation of complex or novel errors to the appropriate specialist
  • Continuous learning from resolved cases to improve future accuracy

 

Key Benefit:

Error queues that historically consumed hours of investigative effort are triaged and partially resolved automatically. The system explains errors in plain language and proposes corrections, dramatically reducing mean time to resolution.

 

 

Note: AI-assisted error resolution for production accounting is already "Delivered - Awaiting Publishing" with Joule integration for event-based posting errors.

 

 

Scenario 4: AI-Assisted Allocation Run Results

 

 

Current Challenge

Cost allocations in S/4HANA can involve thousands of sender-receiver combinations across cost centers, profit centers, and profitability segments. After each allocation run, accountants must review results to confirm they are reasonable — typically by exporting data to spreadsheets, comparing against prior periods, and manually investigating any anomalies. This is tedious, error-prone, and often done under time pressure at period end.

Challenge Area

Impact

Volume of allocation results

Thousands of line items to review per run

Manual comparison to benchmarks

Prior period comparison done in spreadsheets

Lack of explanation

System shows numbers but not reasons for deviations

Time pressure

Reviews compressed into tight close windows

Inconsistent review depth

Different reviewers apply different standards

 

Process Flow: Before and After AI

 

 

Top: Current spreadsheet-based review. Bottom: Joule-powered AI surfaces anomalies and explanations.

Where the AI Agent Intervenes

AI-Assisted Allocation Run Results uses Joule to:

  • Provide amounts allocated within selected fiscal period, year, and ledger
  • Show allocations between cost objects, profit centers, or profitability objects within the universal allocation space
  • Drill down to details such as run ID for specific allocation cycles
  • Flag anomalies against historical benchmarks automatically
  • Provide natural language explanations of why results deviate from expectations
  • Enable conversational exploration: "Show me the top 5 cost centers with the largest allocation increase vs. last period"

 

Key Benefit:

Instead of manually scanning thousands of allocation results for outliers, accountants receive a curated, AI-analyzed view of what requires attention — with context and explanations built in.

 

 

This is the most mature of the five scenarios and is available for adoption today.

 

 

Scenario 5: Intelligent Situation Handling

 

 

Current Challenge

In complex finance operations, exceptions and anomalies occur continuously — failed postings, cash flow deviations, compliance threshold breaches, SLA violations. Today, these situations often sit unnoticed in system queues until someone discovers them during manual reviews or, worse, during audit. The reactive model means problems compound, timelines slip, and risk exposure increases.

Challenge Area

Impact

Delayed detection

Issues sit in queues for hours or days before discovery

Reactive response

Teams only learn about problems during manual reviews

No prioritization

All exceptions treated equally regardless of business impact

Manual triage

Humans must assess and route every exception individually

Recurring patterns

Same issues recur without systematic prevention

Audit exposure

Undetected situations create compliance risk

 

Process Flow: Before and After AI

 

 

Top: Reactive discovery of issues. Bottom: Real-time detection, classification, and autonomous resolution.

Where the AI Agent Intervenes

Intelligent Situation Automation (CA-SIT-ATM) is a built-in S/4HANA capability that:

  • Detects business-critical situations in real time as they occur
  • Classifies situations by severity, type, and required response
  • Triggers automated responses for routine situations (e.g., retry failed postings)
  • Escalates critical situations to the right person with full context
  • Learns from resolution patterns to improve future detection and response
  • Provides dashboards for monitoring situation volumes, resolution times, and trends

 

Key Benefit:

Finance teams move from reactive firefighting to proactive governance. The system watches, alerts, and in many cases resolves — humans make the judgment calls on what truly requires their attention.

Implementation Timeline

Status
Available Today

Target Timeline
Current Release (GA)

Availability
SAP S/4HANA Cloud & Private Edition — Component CA-SIT-ATM

 

Intelligent Situation Handling is already a standard component in SAP S/4HANA. It can be configured and activated for finance scenarios today.

 

 

Joule: Your Conversational Finance Co-Pilot

 

 

At the center of SAP's AI experience is Joule — SAP's generative AI assistant. Joule is not a chatbot bolted onto the side of your ERP. It is deeply integrated into S/4HANA processes, understanding your data context, your role, and your intent.

For finance users, Joule enables:

  • Natural language queries across financial data — ask about open items, aging balances, or variance explanations in plain language
  • Guided transaction execution — initiate journal entries, run reports, or navigate complex closing tasks through conversation
  • Contextual insights — receive proactive recommendations based on your current workflow and the state of your financial data
  • Allocation analysis — explore allocation run results conversationally (available now)
  • Error explanations — get plain-language descriptions of posting errors and recommended fixes

 https://www.sap.com/india/products/artificial-intelligence/ai-assistant/finance.html

 

Joule represents the shift from "learning the system" to "the system learning you." For finance teams stretched thin during close periods, this is not convenience — it is capacity.

 

 

Why Finance Professionals Should Pay Attention Now

The Compound Effect of Autonomous Agents

The true power of agentic AI emerges when multiple agents operate in concert. Imagine a close cycle where:

  • The accruals agent proposes entries based on current data
  • The reconciliation agent matches intercompany positions in parallel
  • The error resolution agent clears posting exceptions as they arise
  • Intelligent Situation Handling monitors for anomalies across all processes
  • Joule surfaces a consolidated status view and flags items requiring your decision
